title: NASCAR Gets a New CEO: Steve O'Donnell Promises to 'Unite' and Bring Back the Fun
summary: NASCAR is shifting gears. Steve O'Donnell, a 30-year veteran of the racing organization, has been named CEO — the first person outside the founding France family to hold that title. O'Donnell, who was promoted to president just a year ago, now takes the wheel at a crucial moment. 'We lost that in recent years,' O'Donnell said about the fun factor, calling NASCAR a 'badass American sport.' He wants to bring that energy back. The change comes after Jim France stepped down as CEO but remains chairman. Jim France had been in charge since 2019, but his tenure was rocky. He took a hardline stance in negotiations for the 2025 revenue-sharing agreement, which triggered an antitrust lawsuit from Michael Jordan's 23XI Racing and Front Row Motorsports. During the trial, France struggled on the witness stand, and NASCAR Commissioner Steve Phelps resigned after inflammatory texts he sent during negotiations were revealed. O'Donnell escaped the scandal unscathed. Now he's tasked with uniting the industry. 'It's what we have to do each and every day,' O'Donnell said. 'We've got to showcase that.' He promised to listen to every stakeholder — including fans — and address matters with urgency. Ben Kennedy, a France family member, was promoted to chief operating officer. Jim France expressed confidence: 'They're going to take this thing even further.' For NASCAR fans, this could mean a new era of excitement and collaboration.
why_it_matters: This shift shows how even a long‑family‑run sports organization can face legal and reputational crises, and how a new leader’s approach can reshape the culture of a multi‑billion‑dollar industry that young fans love.

questions.question: What does O'Donnell say he wants to bring back to NASCAR?

background_read: NASCAR (the National Association for Stock Car Auto Racing) was founded in 1948 by Bill France Sr., and his family has run it ever since.

In 2024, two race teams, 23XI Racing (co-owned by basketball legend Michael Jordan and driver Denny Hamlin) and Front Row Motorsports, sued NASCAR for violating antitrust laws by forcing teams to accept a revenue-sharing deal they considered unfair.

Jim France, who stepped down as CEO but remains chairman, had taken a hardline stance in negotiations, which triggered the lawsuit and led to internal turmoil including the resignation of a top executive over leaked texts.
Now Steve O'Donnell, who has worked at NASCAR for 30 years, becomes CEO with a promise to heal divisions and refocus on fan experience.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Steve O'Donnell named first non-France family CEO of NASCAR
└─ specific: 'We lost that in recent years' — O'Donnell on fun factor
├─ Antitrust lawsuit from 23XI Racing and Front Row Motorsports
└─ Resignation of NASCAR President Steve Phelps after leaked inflammatory texts
├─ O'Donnell vowing to 'unite the industry', listen to stakeholders
└─ Ben Kennedy (France family member) promoted to COO
END QUOTE: Jim France expresses confidence in the new leadership

perspectives.description: Fans and teams hope O'Donnell’s fresh perspective will restore collaboration and excitement, making NASCAR more fan‑friendly and innovative.
Some critics point out that the ongoing antitrust case could still destabilize NASCAR, and that changing CEOs alone may not heal deep divides with drivers and owners.
The transition marks the end of nearly 80 years of direct France family control, which is a significant historical shift for a sport built around one family’s vision.
If O'Donnell succeeds in uniting the industry, NASCAR could attract new sponsors, younger audiences, and perhaps even new international markets.
